subject: ap-government
title: SCOTUS Comparison — Betts v. Brady (1942)
stimulus:
In 1941, Smith Betts was arrested for robbery in the state of Maryland. Unable to afford a lawyer, he was denied a public defender by a judge and forced to represent himself. He was found guilty and given an eight-year sentence.

In the ensuing case, Betts v. Brady (1942), the Supreme Court ruled against Mr. Betts, who claimed his constitutional right to a fair trial was denied. In a 6-3 decision, the Court held that the right to counsel applied to only criminal cases in federal courts and that he did not have a right to counsel in a state court, except in death penalty cases.

Justice Black, in his dissenting opinion, reasoned, "This case can be determined by a resolution of a narrower question: whether, in view of the nature of the offense and the circumstances of his trial and conviction, this petitioner was denied the procedural protection which is his right under the federal Constitution. I think he was."

parts.prompt: Identify the constitutional amendment in the Bill of Rights that is common to both Gideon v. Wainwright (1963) and Betts v. Brady (1942).
rubric_criteria: 1 pt: Identifies the Sixth Amendment as the constitutional amendment common to both cases.
parts.scoring_notes: Only the Sixth Amendment earns the point. Other amendments do not earn the point.

parts.prompt: Explain how the decision in Gideon v. Wainwright relates to the reasoning in Justice Black's dissenting opinion in Betts v. Brady.
rubric_criteria: 1 pt (first point): Describes relevant information about the decision in the required Supreme Court case Gideon v. Wainwright. Example: In Gideon, the defendant was entitled to a court-appointed attorney or an attorney paid for by the state.

2 pts (second point): Correctly explains how the Gideon decision relates to Justice Black's dissent. Example: Justice Black's dissent in Betts argued that the U.S. Constitution protected the right to an attorney, which influenced the later decision in Gideon v. Wainwright — the Gideon majority effectively adopted Black's dissenting position.
parts.scoring_notes: The first point requires accurate information about Gideon (the required/known case). The second point requires connecting Black's dissent in Betts to the Gideon holding — the dissent anticipated/influenced the Gideon decision.

parts.prompt: Explain how the decision in Betts v. Brady demonstrates the principle of federalism.
rubric_criteria: 1 pt: Explains how the Betts decision demonstrates federalism. Accept: In Betts, the Supreme Court did not incorporate the Sixth Amendment to the states, which reflects how federalism leaves many decisions to the states; the Betts ruling preserved state power over criminal procedure by not requiring states to provide counsel in non-capital cases.
parts.scoring_notes: The response must explain the connection between the Betts ruling and federalism — specifically that the Court's refusal to incorporate the Sixth Amendment to states preserved state power over their own court procedures.
